narrative_blocks.header: She became 'The Empress of Sixteenth Street'
narrative_blocks.body: After her husband's senatorial career ended in 1869, Mary Foote Henderson became a real estate developer and civic force in Washington D.C. She and John bought up lots in the Meridian Hill neighborhood, had architect George Oakley Totten Jr. design nearly a dozen buildings for diplomatic use, and created what became Embassy Row. For forty years she personally appeared before Congressional committees to improve the neighborhood. When she died in 1931, the Commission of Fine Arts praised her: 'Single-handed and alone she appeared before committees of Congress to urge approval...

narrative_blocks.header: She lobbied to replace the White House
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Henderson supported two successive plans to build a colossal presidential mansion on Meridian Hill to replace the White House — architect Paul Pelz's plan in 1898 and Franklin W. Smith's in 1900. She also had a home built on 16th Street and offered it to the government as the Vice President's mansion.

narrative_blocks.header: Her mother Eunice discovered the greenhouse effect
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Foote Henderson was born in 1842, the daughter of Eunice Newton Foote — the scientist who published the first greenhouse effect paper in 1856 — and Judge Elisha Foote Jr. She married Senator John Brooks Henderson, who co-authored the 13th Amendment abolishing slavery. She carried both legacies forward as a suffragist, temperance advocate, author, and the most persistent civic lobbyist in Washington for four decades.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Their 30-room castle on Sixteenth Street, demolished 1949.

cross_connections.display_label: who reigned over Los Angeles’s West Adams as Mary did Meridian Hill

career.organization: (Practical Cooking and Dinner Giving, 1877; The Aristocracy of Health)

narrative_blocks.header: Slave-state senator who wrote abolition amendment
narrative_blocks.body: Henderson represented Missouri, a slave state, in the United States Senate. His proposal of January 11, 1864 became the Thirteenth Amendment thirteen months later.

narrative_blocks.header: One of seven Republicans who acquitted Johnson
narrative_blocks.body: He voted to acquit at the 1868 impeachment trial despite knowing the cost. The vote ended his Senate career as he expected.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Mary Foote weeks after losing his seat
narrative_blocks.body: His new wife was the daughter of Eunice Newton Foote, the Hooker-descendant scientist who first described the greenhouse effect. Henderson married into the tree the year after the impeachment vote.

narrative_blocks.header: First American special prosecutor, fired by his appointer
narrative_blocks.body: Grant named him in 1875 to investigate the Whiskey Ring in Saint Louis. When the trail led to Grant's personal secretary, Grant fired him as 'impertinent.'

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Their 30-room castle on Sixteenth Street, demolished 1949.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandmother discovered the greenhouse effect
narrative_blocks.body: John Brooks Henderson Jr. was the grandson of Eunice Newton Foote, who published the first greenhouse effect paper in 1856. He graduated Harvard 1891, served as private secretary to diplomat John W. Foster, traveled with General Nelson Miles to Europe and the Ottoman Empire, and became a Smithsonian Institution Board of Regents member in 1911.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife died at 35
narrative_blocks.body: After John Jr. and his wife Angelica took in an adopted daughter, Beatrice, a bitter rift followed: in 1931 Beatrice tried to have her grandmother Mary Foote Henderson declared incompetent. Mary retaliated by publicly revealing that Beatrice was adopted and cutting her from the will in favor of her secretary.

narrative_blocks.header: A diplomat who collected Caribbean shells
narrative_blocks.body: John Henderson Jr. turned a boyhood hobby into expertise, mounting expeditions to the West Indies to study marine shell life and donating his collection to the U.S. National Museum. A lizard, Anolis hendersoni, was named in his honor.

career.role: Private Secretary to diplomat John W. Foster

parents.father_research_notes: John Schuyler Crosby, 5th Governor of Montana Territory
parents.mother_research_notes: Harriet Van Rensselaer Crosby (1838-1911); youngest dau. of Gen. Stephen Van Rensselaer IV (last patroon of Rensselaerwyck); granddaughter of Gen. Philip Schuyler. Not a separate entry.

narrative_blocks.header: She died at 35
narrative_blocks.body: Angelica Schuyler Crosby married diplomat John Brooks Henderson Jr. in 1903 in Jefferson County, West Virginia. Unable to have children, they adopted Beatrice Van Rensselaer Henderson. Angelica died in 1907 at approximately 35, leaving Henderson a widower with an infant daughter.

parents.father_research_notes: Adopted daughter of John Brooks Henderson Jr. (HD3150). Biological parents unknown.
parents.mother_research_notes: Adoptive mother X02109 died 1907 — before or around time of Beatrice's birth.

sources.short: Wikipedia — John Henderson Jr.; Mary Foote Henderson

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted into the Henderson name
narrative_blocks.body: Beatrice Van Rensselaer Henderson was adopted by John Brooks Henderson Jr. and raised partly by her formidable grandmother, Mary Foote Henderson. Though not a blood descendant, she carried the family name, married diplomat Joseph Wholean in 1926, and shared fully in the dynasty’s tangled fortunes.

institutions.institution_blurb: His wife Eunice Newton Foote attended Troy Female Seminary 1836-1838; Elisha's work as Patent Commissioner and her scientific papers were published in parallel.

narrative_blocks.header: He became Patent Commissioner and immediately exposed $80,000
narrative_blocks.body: When Elisha Foote became the 11th Commissioner of Patents in July 1868, he began investigating expenditures and found that the office had lost over $80,000 in one year through handshake agreements, over-ordering, under-delivery, and patents granted for money rather than merit. He filed a complaint with the Secretary of the Interior; an investigation confirmed his findings. It was widely believed he would be retained for a second term.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued his own stove patent before
narrative_blocks.body: Elisha Foote patented an automatic draft regulator for stoves — the stove his wife Eunice had actually invented. The patent became the subject of multiple lawsuits that reached the Supreme Court three times, under the caption Foote v. Silsby.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote a work on the calculus
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond law, Elisha Foote was a mathematician and scientist who wrote a treatise on calculus and produced original scientific papers that he presented before scientific societies. He was a founding member of the National Institute of Applied Sciences (1870). His daughter Mary married Senator John B.

narrative_blocks.header: Patent Commissioner and Seneca Falls signer
narrative_blocks.body: U.S. Commissioner of Patents 1868-69. As one of forty men, he signed the Declaration of Sentiments at the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention in support of his wife Eunice Newton Foote and the women's rights movement. His Foote line ran six generations back to Wethersfield founder Nathaniel Foote 1635, joining Almira Foote Cowles and Eli Foote as the three Foote-line in-laws of the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: A patent chief who wrote on calculus
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond patent law, Elisha Foote pursued pure mathematics, authoring a work on the calculus and reading original scientific papers before learned societies. He argued his own stove-patent case, Foote v. Silsby, before the U.S. Supreme Court three separate times.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Hooker-tree spouse from the Nathaniel¹ Foote 1635 line.
Fellow Hooker-tree spouse from the Nathaniel¹ Foote 1635 line.

education.institution_name: Law office of Judge Daniel Cady, Johnstown NY

institutions.institution_blurb: Troy Female Seminary, attended 1836-1838 at age 17-19. Emma Hart Willard (H00597) founded the school; Almira Hart Phelps (H00598), another Hooker descendant, was assistant principal. Eunice also studied at the adjacent Rensselaer School under Amos Eaton — the lab training that directly produced her 1856 greenhouse effect paper.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Hooker descendants at Troy Seminary
cross_connections.description: Both Hooker descendants at Troy Seminary — Phelps as vice-principal, Foote as student who later published the first greenhouse effect paper.

cross_connections.display_label: Willard's Troy Seminary trained her in sciences
cross_connections.description: Emma Hart Willard founded the school where Foote received the scientific training that led to her 1856 greenhouse effect discovery.

cross_connections.display_label: another Newton scientist whose discoveries were credited elsewhere
another woman whose discovery a man was credited for
the next woman to publish in serious physics journals after her

notable.notable_blurb: First to describe the greenhouse effect, 1856

narrative_blocks.header: First paper on the greenhouse effect, 1856
narrative_blocks.body: In 1856, Eunice Newton Foote submitted a paper to the AAAS demonstrating that carbon dioxide absorbs significantly more heat than air or hydrogen, and theorizing that higher CO2 levels would raise the Earth's temperature. This was the first known scientific paper to identify the greenhouse effect. Her paper predated John Tyndall's 1859 work by three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Signed the 1848 Seneca Falls Declaration of Sentiments
narrative_blocks.body: Foote and her husband Elisha were both signatories of the Declaration of Sentiments at the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first gathering focused solely on women's rights. She was one of five women who prepared the proceedings for publication. Elizabeth Cady Stanton was her neighbor; Elisha had purchased the house that the Stanton family moved into in 1847.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied at Troy alongside cousin Almira Phelps
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Newton attended Troy Female Seminary from 1836 to 1838. During her attendance, Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps — Emma Willard's sister and another Thomas Hooker descendant — was assistant principal, teaching science and writing the botany textbooks used in class. Eunice also studied at the adjacent Rensselaer School under Amos Eaton, learning laboratory methods.

narrative_blocks.header: Her inventions patented in her husband's name
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice Foote was a prolific inventor: a thermostatically controlled cooking stove (1842), a rubber boot insert (1860), and a paper-making machine (1864). She told Elizabeth Cady Stanton in 1868 that half of all patents filed were on women's inventions — but men took out the patents because women couldn't defend them in court. Her husband Elisha collected a substantial infringement settlement on the stove patent.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Wethersfield Foote line
narrative_blocks.body: Eunice's 1841 marriage to Elisha Foote folded her into the Foote family that had founded Wethersfield Connecticut in 1635. Her own Hooker descent ran through Mary Hooker and Roger Newton — but her husband's line ran back to Nathaniel Foote, shared with Almira Mills Foote and Eli Foote as the three Foote-line in-laws of the Hooker tree.

education.degree: Scientific and classical curriculum under Emma Willard

burialCemetery.name: Green-Wood Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Section 34, Lot 8379 — John Brooks Henderson (I00287) and wife Mary Foote Henderson (HD3149). Also Eunice Newton Foote (HD3148).
